content.problemStatement:
A **binary** string SSS of length NNN is said to be almost alternating if it can be converted into an alternating†^\dagger† string by performing the following operation **at most once:**


Choose two indices LLL and RRR (1≤L≤R≤N1 \leq L \leq R \leq N1≤L≤R≤N).
Then, reverse the substring of SSS from LLL to RRR.

After this operation, SSS will look like S1S2…SL−1SRSR−1…SL+1SLSR+1…SNS_1S_2\ldots S_{L-1}S_RS_{R-1}\ldots S_{L+1}S_LS_{R+1}\ldots S_NS1​S2​…SL−1​SR​SR−1​…SL+1​SL​SR+1​…SN​.
For example, if S=10110110S = \mathtt{10110110}S=10110110, choosing L=3,R=6L = 3, R = 6L=3,R=6 will result in S=101011‾10S = \mathtt{10 \underline{1011} 10}S=101011​10, with the reversed substring underlined.

You are given a binary string SSS. How many of its substrings are almost alternating?

†^\dagger† A binary string is said to be alternating if no two adjacent characters are equal. For example, 0,101,\mathtt{0}, \mathtt{101},0,101, and 01010\mathtt{01010}01010 are examples of alternating strings, while 00\mathtt{00}00 and 1010110110\mathtt{1010110110}1010110110 are not.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case will contain a single integer NNN, the length of SSS.
The second line will contain the binary string SSS.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: the number of substrings of SSS that can be made alternating after one operation.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The only substrings of 0011\mathtt{0011}0011 that are not almost alternating are 00\mathtt{00}00 and 11\mathtt{11}11.
Test case 222: The almost alternating substrings are 1\mathtt{1}1 (two occurrences), 0\mathtt{0}0 (three occurrences), 10,100,01,001\mathtt{10}, \mathtt{100}, \mathtt{01}, \mathtt{001}10,100,01,001.
